Case title: Human-developed codebooks for policy stakeholder interview coding
Short description: Compare AI coding quality when the codebook is supplied by policy experts versus when AI proposes themes and labels first.
Empirical unit of analysis: Paragraphs from stakeholder interviews plus iterative codebooks and prompt histories
Candidate roles tested: Construct boundary setter; context witness; protocol architect
Key typology question: Is the human role in qualitative coding moving from line-by-line coding to codebook design and exception handling?
Observable artifacts / traces: Codebook revisions, prompt iterations, unmatched-theme logs, adjudication notes
Key outcomes observed: Specificity of themes, policy usability, inter-method agreement, time saved
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: AI-first thematic analysis without human codebook development is equally specific and usable
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Policy interviews may be more structured than ethnographic or highly sensitive data
Why this case has high leverage: Visible codebook and revision traces make the role shift empirically inspectable.
main_limitations: Primary source access is limited; case is usable with explicit caveats and landing-page evidence.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.
